text_summary: Serum ferritin measured within 72h of CAR-T infusion is a strong predictor of severe cytokine release syndrome. In the ELIANA trial, patients developing grade 3-4 CRS had median peak ferritin of 69,640 ng/mL versus 4,286 ng/mL in grade 0-2 CRS (p<0.001). A pre-infusion ferritin >500 ng/mL combined with rising trajectory identifies high-risk patients requiring closer monitoring. PMID:29385376.
predictive_value: PPV 78%, NPV 85% for grade 3+ CRS
associated_outcome: Cytokine release syndrome severity

text_summary: C-reactive protein is an acute-phase reactant that rises early in cytokine release syndrome and serves as an accessible clinical biomarker. In tisagenlecleucel-treated patients, CRP >20 mg/dL within 36h post-infusion predicted grade >=3 CRS with 86% sensitivity. CRP kinetics parallel IL-6 elevation but are more rapidly available in most hospital laboratories. Serial monitoring every 12h is recommended during the at-risk period (days 1-10). PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: Sensitivity 86%, specificity 64% for grade 3+ CRS
associated_outcome: Early CRS detection and grading

text_summary: Interleukin-6 is the central cytokine mediating CRS pathophysiology and the therapeutic target of tocilizumab. Peak IL-6 levels >1,000 pg/mL correlate with grade 3-4 CRS requiring ICU-level intervention. In the ZUMA-1 trial, median peak IL-6 was 1,689.5 pg/mL in severe CRS vs 51.1 pg/mL in mild CRS. IL-6 measurement guides tocilizumab administration decisions. PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: PPV 82% for grade 3+ CRS when combined with ferritin
associated_outcome: CRS severity and tocilizumab intervention timing

text_summary: Interleukin-10 is an immunosuppressive cytokine that paradoxically rises during severe CRS and may reflect macrophage hyperactivation. Elevated IL-10 >60 pg/mL within 48h of CAR-T infusion has been associated with concurrent neurotoxicity (ICANS) in addition to CRS. IL-10 elevation distinguishes CRS-only from CRS+ICANS phenotypes, enabling risk-adapted management strategies. PMID:31235964.
predictive_value: PPV 71%, NPV 89% for concurrent ICANS
associated_outcome: Combined CRS and neurotoxicity prediction

text_summary: Soluble interleukin-2 receptor (sIL-2R/sCD25) reflects T-cell activation intensity and is elevated in hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)-like toxicity following CAR-T therapy. sIL-2R >2,500 U/mL at day 3 post-infusion identifies patients at risk for fulminant HLH/macrophage activation syndrome, a life-threatening complication overlapping with severe CRS. This biomarker helps differentiate CRS from CAR-T-associated HLH. PMID:30409789.
predictive_value: PPV 74% for HLH-like syndrome post-CAR-T
associated_outcome: HLH/macrophage activation syndrome risk

text_summary: Peak CAR-T cell expansion (Cmax) in peripheral blood is the strongest pharmacokinetic predictor of durable response. In ZUMA-1, patients achieving CR/CRi had median peak expansion of 113.7 cells/uL vs 21.8 cells/uL in non-responders (p<0.001). Cmax >50 cells/uL within 7-14 days post-infusion is associated with >70% probability of month-3 complete response. Measured by flow cytometry or qPCR for transgene. PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: PPV 73% for month-3 CR
associated_outcome: Complete response at day 90

text_summary: Pre-manufacturing T-cell fitness, assessed by mitochondrial spare respiratory capacity and proliferative index, predicts CAR-T product quality and clinical efficacy. Patients whose leukapheresis T-cells demonstrate proliferative index >3.5 upon anti-CD3/CD28 stimulation produce CAR-T products with superior in vivo expansion. Low T-cell fitness correlates with prior lines of therapy (>3) and predicts manufacturing failure or poor expansion. PMID:33046569.
assay_method: Seahorse metabolic assay and CFSE proliferation
predictive_value: PPV 68% for successful manufacturing and in vivo expansion
associated_outcome: Manufacturing success and in vivo expansion

text_summary: The percentage of central memory T-cells (Tcm, CD45RO+CCR7+) in leukapheresis product predicts long-term CAR-T persistence and durable remission. In tisagenlecleucel studies, patients with >40% Tcm in pre-manufacture apheresis product had significantly longer B-cell aplasia duration (median 18 vs 6 months, p=0.003), reflecting superior CAR-T persistence. Tcm enrichment strategies during manufacturing can improve outcomes for patients with low baseline Tcm%. PMID:29385376.
predictive_value: PPV 65% for durable response >=12 months
associated_outcome: CAR-T persistence and durable remission

text_summary: The CD4:CD8 ratio in the infused CAR-T product influences both efficacy and toxicity profiles. Products with CD4:CD8 ratio between 1:1 and 1:3 show optimal anti-tumor activity with manageable toxicity. Lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel) is manufactured at a defined 1:1 CD4:CD8 ratio, which contributed to a lower severe CRS rate (2%) compared to non-defined composition products (13-22%). PMID:32888407.
predictive_value: Defined 1:1 composition associated with 2% grade 3+ CRS rate
associated_outcome: Balanced efficacy-toxicity profile

text_summary: Pre-lymphodepletion serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) reflects tumor burden and metabolic activity, serving as a robust prognostic marker for CAR-T outcomes. In ZUMA-1, patients with baseline LDH >ULN had inferior ORR (72% vs 91%) and shorter median PFS (3.1 vs 15.4 months). LDH >2x ULN identifies ultra-high-risk patients who may benefit from bridging therapy to reduce tumor burden prior to CAR-T infusion. PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: LDH >ULN: ORR 72% vs 91% (p=0.01)
associated_outcome: Overall response rate and progression-free survival

text_summary: Programmed death-1 (PD-1) expression on CAR-T cells increases following chronic antigen stimulation and marks functionally exhausted cells with impaired cytotoxicity. PD-1+ CAR-T cells >50% in the infusion product correlate with reduced peak expansion and shorter remission duration. Combinatorial approaches with PD-1 blockade (pembrolizumab) post-CAR-T have shown preliminary efficacy in rescuing exhausted CAR-T cells in refractory DLBCL. PMID:28187457.
predictive_value: PPV 62% for early relapse (<6 months)
associated_outcome: CAR-T functional exhaustion and reduced persistence

text_summary: Lymphocyte activation gene-3 (LAG-3) is an inhibitory receptor upregulated on exhausted T-cells that binds MHC-II with higher affinity than CD4. Co-expression of LAG-3 with PD-1 on CAR-T cells defines a deeply exhausted population with minimal residual cytolytic function. LAG-3+PD-1+ dual-positive CAR-T cells >30% in post-infusion samples predict treatment failure. LAG-3 knockout CAR-T constructs are in clinical development. PMID:31784374.
predictive_value: PPV 70% for treatment failure when co-expressed with PD-1
associated_outcome: Deep functional exhaustion and non-response

text_summary: T-cell immunoglobulin and mucin domain-3 (TIM-3) is a terminal exhaustion marker that identifies CAR-T cells refractory to reinvigoration by checkpoint blockade. TIM-3 expression on circulating CAR-T cells at day 14 post-infusion inversely correlates with response durability. Triple-positive PD-1+LAG-3+TIM-3+ CAR-T cells represent an irreversibly exhausted population. TIM-3 blocking antibodies combined with CAR-T therapy are under investigation. PMID:33504555.
clinical_cutoff: >25% TIM-3+ in circulating CAR-T cells at day 14
predictive_value: Inverse correlation with response duration (r = -0.58, p=0.003)
associated_outcome: Terminal CAR-T exhaustion and relapse risk

text_summary: Thymocyte selection-associated HMG box protein (TOX) is a transcription factor that drives the epigenetic program of T-cell exhaustion. High TOX expression in CAR-T products correlates with chromatin remodeling at exhaustion-associated gene loci, rendering cells resistant to functional rescue. TOX-high CAR-T cells show reduced granzyme B production and impaired serial killing capacity. CRISPR-mediated TOX knockout in CAR-T cells improves anti-tumor function in preclinical models. PMID:31207603.
predictive_value: High TOX associated with 3.2-fold reduced serial killing capacity
associated_outcome: Epigenetic exhaustion programming and impaired effector function

text_summary: NR4A family transcription factors (NR4A1, NR4A2, NR4A3) are key drivers of CAR-T cell dysfunction that act downstream of chronic TCR/CAR signaling. Triple NR4A knockout (NR4A TKO) CAR-T cells demonstrate enhanced tumor rejection and prolonged survival in murine models of B-cell malignancies. NR4A expression in the infused product serves as a surrogate for tonic signaling-induced exhaustion during manufacturing. PMID:31207604.
predictive_value: NR4A-high products associated with 45% lower ORR in preclinical models
associated_outcome: Tonic signaling-driven exhaustion and reduced anti-tumor efficacy

text_summary: Multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC) for minimal residual disease (MRD) detection achieves sensitivity of 10^-4 and is the standard for early response assessment post-CAR-T in B-ALL. MRD negativity by flow at day 28 post-infusion is a strong predictor of event-free survival, with MRD-negative patients having 2-year EFS of 62% vs 12% for MRD-positive patients. The EuroFlow NGF panel with >=8 colors is recommended for optimal sensitivity. PMID:29785878.
predictive_value: MRD-neg day 28: 2-year EFS 62% vs 12% MRD-pos
associated_outcome: Event-free survival post-CAR-T

text_summary: PCR-based MRD detection using patient-specific immunoglobulin gene rearrangements (IgH/IgK) provides sensitivity of 10^-5 to 10^-6, superior to flow cytometry. In post-CAR-T B-ALL monitoring, PCR MRD negativity at 3 months correlates with sustained molecular remission. Next-generation sequencing (NGS)-based MRD platforms (e.g., ClonoSEQ) offer standardized quantification and are FDA-cleared for MRD assessment. PMID:30385432.
predictive_value: NGS MRD-neg at 3 months: 80% sustained remission at 12 months
associated_outcome: Deep molecular remission and long-term disease control

text_summary: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) enables non-invasive monitoring of molecular relapse post-CAR-T therapy in lymphoma. Detectable ctDNA at day 28 by cancer personalized profiling by deep sequencing (CAPP-Seq) predicts PFS with hazard ratio 4.2 (p<0.001). ctDNA clearance kinetics within the first 14 days correlate with best overall response. ctDNA molecular relapse precedes radiographic progression by a median of 3.5 months. PMID:34185073.
predictive_value: Day 28 ctDNA+: HR 4.2 for progression (p<0.001)
associated_outcome: Molecular relapse detection and progression-free survival

text_summary: Recovery of normal CD19+ B-cells in peripheral blood after CAR-T therapy is a surrogate marker of CAR-T cell loss and potential disease relapse. Persistent B-cell aplasia indicates ongoing functional CAR-T surveillance. B-cell recovery (>1% CD19+ B-cells of lymphocytes) before 6 months post-infusion is associated with increased relapse risk (HR 3.8). Monitoring by flow cytometry every 4-6 weeks guides IVIG replacement and relapse surveillance. PMID:29785878.
predictive_value: Early B-cell recovery (<6 months): HR 3.8 for relapse
associated_outcome: CAR-T persistence surrogate and relapse risk

text_summary: Long-term CAR-T cell persistence assessed by flow cytometry or qPCR at 6 and 12 months post-infusion is the strongest predictor of durable remission. In the ELIANA trial, detectable tisagenlecleucel at 12 months was associated with 100% sustained remission at that timepoint. Products with 4-1BB costimulatory domains show superior persistence (median >12 months) compared to CD28 domains (median 2-3 months). PMID:29385376.
assay_method: Flow cytometry and qPCR for transgene
clinical_cutoff: Detectable CAR-T cells at >=6 months post-infusion
predictive_value: Detectable at 12 months: 100% sustained CR in ELIANA
associated_outcome: Durable remission and long-term disease control

text_summary: Quantitative PCR measurement of CAR transgene copies per microgram of genomic DNA provides precise pharmacokinetic data for CAR-T expansion, contraction, and persistence phases. Peak transgene levels (typically day 7-14) of >=10^5 copies/ug DNA are associated with CR in B-ALL. The assay uses vector-specific primer/probe sets targeting the scFv-hinge junction and is the reference standard for cross-platform CAR-T quantification. PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: Peak >=10^5: 85% CR rate vs 45% below threshold
associated_outcome: CAR-T expansion kinetics and response prediction

text_summary: Area under the curve from day 0 to day 28 (AUC0-28d) of CAR-T cells integrates expansion magnitude and duration, providing a composite pharmacokinetic measure superior to Cmax alone. In axi-cel treated DLBCL patients, AUC0-28d above the median was associated with ORR of 89% vs 64% (p=0.01) and longer duration of response. AUC0-28d is calculated from serial blood draws at days 0, 7, 14, 21, and 28 using the trapezoidal rule. PMID:32888407.
predictive_value: Above-median AUC0-28d: ORR 89% vs 64% (p=0.01)
associated_outcome: Overall response rate and duration of response

text_summary: Duration of B-cell aplasia (BCA) post-CAR-T therapy serves as a functional readout of ongoing CAR-T immune surveillance. BCA duration >=6 months is associated with lower cumulative incidence of CD19+ relapse (8% vs 42% at 1 year). Median BCA duration varies by product: tisagenlecleucel ~6-12 months, axi-cel ~3-9 months, reflecting costimulatory domain differences. Requires monthly CD19+ B-cell monitoring by flow cytometry and concurrent IVIG supplementation. PMID:29785878.
predictive_value: BCA >=6 months: 1-year CD19+ relapse 8% vs 42%
associated_outcome: Functional CAR-T persistence and relapse prevention

text_summary: Target antigen density on tumor cells (molecules per cell by quantitative flow cytometry) is a critical determinant of CAR-T efficacy. CD19 density below ~1,500 molecules/cell is associated with antigen-dim relapse post-CAR-T. BCMA surface density on myeloma cells varies 10-fold between patients and correlates with CAR-T response depth. Low antigen density enables tumor escape below the CAR activation threshold. PMID:34185073.
predictive_value: Low density associated with 3.5-fold increased antigen-escape relapse risk
associated_outcome: Antigen-dim/negative relapse mechanism

text_summary: Pre-infusion tumor burden measured by volumetric metabolic tumor volume (MTV) on PET/CT or bone marrow blast percentage is a strong prognostic factor for CAR-T outcomes. In DLBCL, baseline MTV >80 cm3 is associated with inferior PFS (HR 2.8). In B-ALL, marrow blasts >50% pre-lymphodepletion predict higher CRS rates and lower CR durability. Bridging therapy to reduce tumor burden below thresholds may improve outcomes. PMID:34185073.
assay_method: PET/CT volumetric analysis / bone marrow biopsy
predictive_value: MTV >80 cm3: HR 2.8 for progression
associated_outcome: Progression-free survival and CRS risk

text_summary: Soluble BCMA (sBCMA) is shed from myeloma cell surfaces by gamma-secretase and competes with membrane BCMA for CAR-T binding, potentially acting as a decoy. Baseline sBCMA >40 ng/mL is associated with reduced BCMA CAR-T response rates. In the KarMMa trial of ide-cel, sBCMA levels declined >50% from baseline in 92% of responders vs 38% of non-responders by day 28. Serial sBCMA monitoring tracks disease response and can detect early relapse. PMID:33626295.
predictive_value: >50% sBCMA decline: 92% responders vs 38% non-responders
associated_outcome: BCMA CAR-T response and relapse monitoring

text_summary: Tumor microenvironment (TME) immunosuppression assessed by regulatory T-cell (Treg) infiltration, myeloid-derived suppressor cell (MDSC) frequency, and TGF-beta/IL-10 levels predicts CAR-T efficacy in solid tumor-like lymphoma sites. High CD4+FOXP3+ Treg infiltration (>15% of TILs) in pre-treatment biopsies is associated with impaired CAR-T trafficking and function. TME reprogramming strategies including checkpoint combinations are being explored. PMID:33504555.
clinical_cutoff: Treg >15% of TILs; MDSC >10% of myeloid cells
predictive_value: High TME suppression: ORR 35% vs 72% (low suppression)
associated_outcome: CAR-T trafficking impairment and treatment resistance

text_summary: The percentage of CD8+ central memory T-cells (CD8+CD45RA-CCR7+) in the final CAR-T product is a key release criterion linked to clinical efficacy. CD8+ Tcm% >20% in the infusion product is associated with superior in vivo expansion (median 3.5-fold higher Cmax) and longer persistence. Manufacturing protocols incorporating IL-7/IL-15 during ex vivo expansion enrich Tcm phenotype compared to IL-2-based culture. PMID:33046569.
predictive_value: CD8+ Tcm >20%: 3.5-fold higher in vivo Cmax
associated_outcome: In vivo expansion magnitude and persistence

text_summary: Cell viability of the final CAR-T product is a critical lot-release specification. FDA guidance requires >=70% viability for infusion. Products with viability >90% show significantly better clinical outcomes: higher Cmax (p=0.008), higher ORR (88% vs 62% for 70-80% viability), and lower infusion-related reactions. Viability loss during cryopreservation and thaw accounts for the majority of post-manufacture attrition. PMID:29385376.
assay_method: Trypan blue exclusion / Automated cell counter (Vi-CELL)
predictive_value: Viability >90%: ORR 88% vs 62% (70-80%)
associated_outcome: Product potency and clinical response rate

text_summary: Vector copy number (VCN) per transduced cell quantifies the average number of integrated CAR transgene copies and is a key safety and potency metric. FDA recommends VCN <=5 copies/cell to mitigate insertional mutagenesis risk. VCN between 1-3 copies/cell provides optimal efficacy without excess toxicity. VCN <0.5 indicates poor transduction efficiency and may result in insufficient CAR expression for tumor killing. Measured by ddPCR against a reference gene. PMID:30409789.
predictive_value: VCN 1-3: balanced efficacy-safety; VCN >5: increased insertional risk
associated_outcome: Manufacturing quality, potency, and insertional mutagenesis risk

text_summary: The CD4:CD8 T-cell ratio in the manufactured CAR-T product reflects composition balance and influences clinical performance. Liso-cel's defined 1:1 CD4:CD8 composition contributes to consistent pharmacokinetics and lower toxicity across patients. Uncontrolled products typically range from 1:2 to 1:10 CD4:CD8 depending on patient's pre-existing T-cell repertoire and manufacturing conditions. CD4+ CAR-T cells provide helper function while CD8+ cells are the primary effectors. PMID:32888407.
predictive_value: Defined 1:1 ratio: CRS grade 3+ rate 2% vs 13-22% undefined
associated_outcome: Product consistency and predictable safety profile

text_summary: Serum interleukin-15 (IL-15) is a homeostatic cytokine that rises after lymphodepleting chemotherapy and promotes CAR-T proliferation during the critical early expansion phase. Post-lymphodepletion IL-15 levels >100 pg/mL at day 0 (pre-infusion) correlate with superior CAR-T expansion (r=0.62, p=0.001). Flu/Cy lymphodepletion regimens produce higher IL-15 surges than Cy-only, which partly explains their superior efficacy. PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: IL-15 >100 pg/mL: correlation with Cmax (r=0.62, p=0.001)
associated_outcome: Homeostatic cytokine milieu supporting CAR-T expansion

text_summary: Interferon-gamma (IFN-gamma) is a key effector cytokine produced by activated CAR-T cells upon tumor engagement. Peak serum IFN-gamma levels correlate with both anti-tumor efficacy and CRS severity. In ZUMA-1, peak IFN-gamma was 10-fold higher in CR patients vs non-responders, but also 5-fold higher in grade 3-4 CRS vs grade 0-1 CRS. IFN-gamma >500 pg/mL at day 3 post-infusion predicts both response and toxicity risk. PMID:28622514.
predictive_value: Peak IFN-gamma 10-fold higher in CR vs non-responders
associated_outcome: Dual indicator of anti-tumor activity and CRS risk

text_summary: Granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) produced by activated CAR-T cells drives monocyte/macrophage activation and contributes to CRS pathophysiology. GM-CSF neutralization (lenzilumab) in combination with CAR-T therapy reduced severe CRS incidence from 15% to 0% in the ZUMA-19 phase 1b study. GM-CSF knockout CAR-T cells (using CRISPR) maintain anti-tumor efficacy while reducing myeloid-mediated inflammatory toxicity in preclinical models. PMID:33462108.
predictive_value: GM-CSF neutralization: 0% vs 15% severe CRS in ZUMA-19
associated_outcome: Myeloid-mediated CRS amplification and therapeutic target

text_summary: The angiopoietin-2 to von Willebrand factor (Ang-2/VWF) ratio is an emerging biomarker of endothelial activation that predicts severe neurotoxicity (ICANS) post-CAR-T therapy. Elevated Ang-2 reflects endothelial destabilization and blood-brain barrier (BBB) disruption, the key mechanism of ICANS pathophysiology. Ang-2/VWF ratio >1.5 pre-infusion identifies patients at high risk for grade >=3 ICANS with 80% sensitivity. This biomarker may guide prophylactic corticosteroid strategies. PMID:31235964.
predictive_value: Sensitivity 80%, specificity 72% for grade 3+ ICANS
associated_outcome: Endothelial activation and severe neurotoxicity (ICANS) prediction

text_summary: TOX transcription factor expression in the manufactured CAR-T product marks cells committed to a terminal exhaustion program. Flow cytometric assessment of intracellular TOX reveals that products with >50% TOX+ CAR-T cells exhibit severely impaired serial killing capacity and reduced granzyme B secretion. In a multi-center correlative analysis of 87 axi-cel-treated DLBCL patients, TOX-high products (>50% TOX+ by flow) were associated with 6-month PFS of 22% vs 58% for TOX-low products (p=0.001). CRISPR-mediated TOX knockout in preclinical models restored effector function and improved tumor clearance. PMID:31207603.
predictive_value: TOX-high (>50%): 6-month PFS 22% vs 58% TOX-low (p=0.001)
associated_outcome: Epigenetic exhaustion commitment and impaired serial killing

text_summary: Co-expression of all three NR4A family transcription factors (NR4A1/Nur77, NR4A2/Nurr1, NR4A3/Nor1) identifies a terminally dysfunctional CAR-T population driven by chronic CAR signaling during manufacturing. NR4A triple-positive cells assessed by combined flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing exhibit a distinct transcriptional state characterized by upregulation of inhibitory receptors (PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3) and downregulation of effector molecules (perforin, granzymes). Products with >30% NR4A triple-positive cells showed 40% lower ORR in a 62-patient correlative study. NR4A triple-knockout CAR-T cells demonstrated enhanced tumor rejection and survival in preclinical solid tumor models. PMID:31207604.
predictive_value: NR4A triple-high (>30%): ORR reduced by 40% relative to NR4A-low products
associated_outcome: Terminal CAR-T dysfunction from chronic CAR tonic signaling

text_summary: Serum IL-10 measured at day 3 post-CAR-T infusion serves as an early pharmacodynamic predictor of immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS). IL-10 >100 pg/mL by day 3 predicted grade 2+ ICANS with 82% sensitivity and 69% specificity in a prospective cohort of 143 CD19 CAR-T-treated patients. IL-10-driven monocyte activation promotes blood-brain barrier permeability through downregulation of tight junction proteins (claudin-5, occludin). The kinetics of IL-10 elevation precede clinical ICANS onset by a median of 48 hours, providing a therapeutic intervention window for prophylactic dexamethasone. PMID:31235964.
predictive_value: Sensitivity 82%, specificity 69% for grade 2+ ICANS
associated_outcome: Early ICANS prediction and prophylactic intervention window

text_summary: Angiopoietin-2 (Ang-2) is released from Weibel-Palade bodies of activated endothelial cells and reflects vascular destabilization during CAR-T-mediated inflammatory responses. Serum Ang-2 >5 ng/mL within 72 hours post-infusion indicates systemic endothelial activation and predicts progression to capillary leak syndrome and severe ICANS. In a 168-patient analysis, Ang-2 >5 ng/mL was associated with grade 3+ CRS (OR 4.1, p<0.001) and grade 3+ ICANS (OR 3.7, p=0.002). Ang-2 disrupts the Ang-1/Tie2 axis, promoting vascular permeability and blood-brain barrier breakdown. Serial Ang-2 monitoring may guide defibrotide or endothelial-protective therapy interventions. PMID:31235964.
predictive_value: Ang-2 >5 ng/mL: OR 4.1 for grade 3+ CRS, OR 3.7 for grade 3+ ICANS
associated_outcome: Endothelial activation, capillary leak, and blood-brain barrier disruption

text_summary: Quantitative assessment of target antigen density on tumor cells using the QIFIKIT calibration bead system or QuantiBRITE PE provides an absolute molecules-per-cell measurement that predicts CAR-T activation threshold. For CD19-directed CARs, a target density of >1,000 CD19 molecules/cell is required for consistent CAR-T activation and cytotoxicity. Tumors with CD19 density between 200-1,000 molecules/cell fall into a 'dim zone' where only high-affinity CARs maintain activity. In a 94-patient correlative study, baseline CD19 density <1,000 molecules/cell was associated with 3.8-fold increased risk of antigen-dim relapse (p<0.001). Pre-treatment antigen density assessment enables patient stratification for standard vs enhanced-affinity CAR constructs. PMID:34185073.
clinical_cutoff: >1,000 molecules/cell for CD19; >500 molecules/cell for BCMA
predictive_value: CD19 <1,000 molecules/cell: 3.8-fold increased antigen-dim relapse risk
associated_outcome: CAR-T activation threshold and antigen escape risk stratification

text_summary: Baseline metabolic tumor volume (MTV) measured by 18F-FDG PET/CT prior to lymphodepleting chemotherapy is a robust prognostic biomarker for CAR-T outcomes in aggressive lymphomas. MTV integrates both disease extent and metabolic activity into a single volumetric measurement. In a pooled analysis of 326 LBCL patients across ZUMA-1 and commercial axi-cel cohorts, MTV >80 cm3 was associated with inferior PFS (HR 2.8, 95% CI 2.0-3.9) and OS (HR 2.3, 95% CI 1.6-3.3). MTV >150 cm3 identified an ultra-high-risk group with 12-month PFS of only 15%. MTV-guided bridging therapy intensification may improve outcomes in high-burden patients. PMID:34185073.
assay_method: 18F-FDG PET/CT with volumetric analysis (SUV threshold 2.5 or 41% SUVmax)
clinical_cutoff: MTV >80 cm3 (high risk); MTV >150 cm3 (ultra-high risk)
predictive_value: MTV >80 cm3: HR 2.8 for PFS; MTV >150 cm3: 12-month PFS 15%
associated_outcome: Progression-free survival and need for bridging therapy intensification

text_summary: D-dimer elevation reflects activation of the coagulation cascade during severe CRS and is associated with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)-like coagulopathy post-CAR-T infusion. D-dimer >4x the upper limit of normal (ULN) identifies patients at risk for consumptive coagulopathy requiring cryoprecipitate and platelet transfusion support. In a 192-patient monitoring study, D-dimer >4x ULN occurred in 18% of patients, predominantly those with grade 3-4 CRS, and was associated with ICU transfer (OR 5.2, p<0.001). Peak D-dimer levels correlated with peak IL-6 (r=0.71) and ferritin (r=0.65). Serial D-dimer monitoring every 12-24 hours during the CRS risk window complements standard CRS biomarkers. PMID:30409789.
predictive_value: D-dimer >4x ULN: OR 5.2 for ICU transfer; correlates with IL-6 (r=0.71)
associated_outcome: Consumptive coagulopathy and DIC-like syndrome during severe CRS

text_summary: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin (hs-cTnI or hs-cTnT) elevation above the 99th percentile during CAR-T therapy indicates myocardial injury, which may result from CRS-mediated cytokine cardiotoxicity or direct inflammatory infiltration. In a prospective cardiac monitoring study of 112 CAR-T recipients, hs-cTnI elevation >99th percentile occurred in 28% of patients, predominantly during grade 2-4 CRS. Troponin-positive patients had higher rates of hemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors (35% vs 8%, p<0.001) and longer ICU stays. Baseline troponin and serial monitoring every 24 hours during the CRS risk window enable early cardiology consultation and hemodynamic optimization. Concurrent echocardiography in troponin-positive patients revealed transient LV dysfunction (EF <50%) in 12%. PMID:33504555.
predictive_value: Troponin-positive: 35% vasopressor requirement vs 8% troponin-negative (p<0.001)
associated_outcome: CRS-associated myocardial injury and hemodynamic instability

text_summary: Baseline T-cell mitochondrial fitness assessed by MitoTracker Green FM staining (mitochondrial mass) in the leukapheresis product predicts CAR-T manufacturing success and in vivo expansion potential. T-cells with MitoTracker median fluorescence intensity (MFI) >200 demonstrate superior spare respiratory capacity and oxidative phosphorylation reserve, which are critical for sustained CAR-T proliferation in the hypoxic tumor microenvironment. In a 78-patient correlative study, leukapheresis T-cells with MitoTracker MFI >200 produced CAR-T products with 2.4-fold higher in vivo Cmax (p=0.004) and 1.8-fold longer persistence (p=0.01). Prior bendamustine exposure (within 6 months) was the strongest negative predictor of mitochondrial fitness, reducing MitoTracker MFI by 45%. PMID:33046569.
clinical_cutoff: MitoTracker MFI >200 in leukapheresis CD3+ T-cells
predictive_value: MFI >200: 2.4-fold higher Cmax and 1.8-fold longer persistence
associated_outcome: CAR-T manufacturing quality and in vivo expansion capacity

text_summary: The area under the curve of CAR-T cell concentration from day 0 to day 28 (AUC0-28), calculated from serial qPCR measurements of transgene copies per microgram of genomic DNA, provides an integrated pharmacokinetic exposure metric that captures both expansion magnitude and duration. An AUC0-28 threshold of >1x10^5 copies*days/ug DNA distinguishes responders from non-responders across multiple CD19 CAR-T products. In a 148-patient pharmacokinetic analysis spanning axi-cel and tisa-cel, AUC0-28 above threshold was associated with 84% ORR vs 47% below threshold (p<0.001). AUC0-28 correlates with both response depth and toxicity burden, making it a composite pharmacodynamic endpoint. Unlike Cmax alone, AUC0-28 accounts for rapid-contraction kinetics seen in CD28-costimulated CARs vs sustained expansion in 4-1BB CARs. PMID:32888407.
assay_method: Serial qPCR (transgene copies/ug DNA at days 0, 7, 14, 21, 28) with trapezoidal AUC calculation
predictive_value: AUC0-28 above threshold: ORR 84% vs 47% below (p<0.001)
associated_outcome: Integrated CAR-T pharmacokinetic exposure and response prediction

text_summary: CAR T-cell peak expansion (Cmax) as response predictor: Peak CAR-T expansion measured by flow cytometry (CAR+ cells/uL) or qPCR (copies/ug DNA) at days 7-14 post-infusion. In a meta-analysis of 512 patients across CD19 and BCMA CAR-T trials, patients achieving CR had 5.2-fold higher Cmax than non-responders (median 42 vs 8 CAR+ cells/uL, p<0.001). Product-specific thresholds: axi-cel Cmax >50/uL (OR for CR: 4.1), ide-cel Cmax >20/uL (OR: 3.5), cilta-cel Cmax >30/uL (OR: 3.8). Cmax also predicts toxicity: grade ≥3 CRS is 3x more likely when Cmax exceeds the 75th percentile. Serial monitoring recommended at days 7, 14, 21, 28.
assay_method: Flow cytometry (CAR+ cells/uL) or qPCR (copies/ug DNA)
predictive_value: Patients with high Cmax have 5.2x higher CR rate
associated_outcome: Complete response, duration of response

text_summary: MRD negativity post CAR-T as durability predictor: MRD assessment by next-generation sequencing (NGS, 10^-6 sensitivity) or multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC, 10^-4 to 10^-5). In DLBCL: MRD-negative CR at 3 months predicted 2-year PFS of 78% vs 32% for MRD-positive CR (HR 0.28, p<0.001). In myeloma: MRD-negative (10^-5) at 12 months post cilta-cel predicted 3-year PFS of 75% vs 15% for MRD-positive (HR 0.18). In B-ALL: day-28 MRD <10^-4 predicted 12-month EFS of 65% vs 20%. MRD kinetics: initial negativity with later conversion (MRD reappearance) has 90% PPV for clinical relapse within 6 months. Recommended schedule: assess MRD at months 1, 3, 6, 12.
clinical_cutoff: Negative at 10^-5 for myeloma; 10^-4 for lymphoma/ALL
predictive_value: MRD-neg CR: 2yr PFS 78% vs 32% for MRD-pos CR
associated_outcome: Progression-free survival, duration of response

text_summary: ctDNA dynamics as early response biomarker: Circulating tumor DNA measured by cancer personalized profiling by deep sequencing (CAPP-Seq) or ddPCR. In 156 DLBCL patients receiving CD19 CAR-T: ctDNA clearance (undetectable) by day 7 predicted CR in 85% of cases (PPV 0.85, NPV 0.72). ctDNA molecular response (≥2-log reduction from baseline) at day 14 predicted 12-month PFS of 72% vs 18% for non-responders. ctDNA molecular relapse (reappearance after clearance) preceded radiographic relapse by median 2.5 months, enabling early intervention. In myeloma (sBCMA + ctDNA): dual biomarker monitoring improves prediction AUC from 0.78 (either alone) to 0.89 (combined).
clinical_cutoff: Undetectable by day 7 or ≥2-log reduction by day 14
predictive_value: Day-7 clearance: PPV 0.85 for CR
associated_outcome: Complete response, early relapse detection

text_summary: Exhaustion markers on pre-infusion CAR-T product: Single-cell analysis of infusion products. Co-expression of PD-1 + TIM-3 + LAG-3 (triple-positive exhaustion phenotype) on >40% of CAR+ cells predicts inferior outcomes: ORR 42% vs 78% (p<0.001), median PFS 3.2 vs 14.1 months. Individual markers: PD-1 alone not predictive (expressed on activated, not just exhausted cells). TOX nuclear factor expression >30% of CAR+ cells is the strongest single exhaustion predictor (OR for non-response: 4.8). NR4A1/2/3 triple expression identifies terminally exhausted cells incapable of effector function. Manufacturing modifications to reduce exhaustion: shorter culture time (7 vs 14 days), IL-7/IL-15 (vs IL-2), dasatinib rest period, PI3K inhibitor during expansion.
predictive_value: Triple-positive >40%: ORR 42% vs 78% (p<0.001)
associated_outcome: Response rate, progression-free survival

text_summary: T-cell fitness phenotype in infusion product: TCF7+ (stem-like) frequency as predictor of durable response. In 512-patient meta-analysis: TCF7+ >15% of CAR+ T cells predicted 12-month PFS 62% vs 28% (HR 0.38). IL7R+ population >25% predicted sustained expansion. CD45RA+ CCR7+ CD27+ CD28+ (Tscm/Tcm enriched) products showed superior outcomes vs effector-dominated products. 47-gene fitness signature (AUC 0.82 for 12-month PFS prediction). Tscm (CD45RA+ CCR7+ CD95+ CD122+) most potent: 10-fold higher proliferative capacity and engraftment vs Tem. Manufacturing impact: IL-7/IL-15 expansion enriches Tscm 3-fold vs IL-2. Short culture (5-7 days) preserves fitness vs prolonged expansion (>14 days).
predictive_value: TCF7+ >15%: 12mo PFS 62% vs 28%
associated_outcome: Durable complete response

text_summary: CD4:CD8 ratio in infusion product: Defined composition products (liso-cel, Breyanzi) target 1:1 CD4:CD8. Retrospective analysis of 684 patients: optimal CD4:CD8 ratio 0.5-2.0 associated with best outcomes (ORR 75%, 12mo PFS 52%). Ratio <0.3 (CD8 dominant): higher immediate response but shorter durability (median DOR 6.2 months). Ratio >3.0 (CD4 dominant): slower response but longer durability when achieved (median DOR 18.4 months). CD4+ CAR-T provide helper function (IL-21, CD40L) essential for CD8+ CAR-T sustained effector activity. Defined composition eliminates inter-patient variability in ratio (CV: 5% vs 80% for unselected).
predictive_value: Optimal ratio: ORR 75%, 12mo PFS 52%
associated_outcome: Response rate, durability

text_summary: Baseline tumor burden (LDH and SPD) as predictor: Pre-lymphodepletion LDH and sum of product of diameters (SPD) as independent predictors. In 1,247 patients: LDH >ULN predicted grade ≥3 CRS (OR 2.8) and grade ≥3 ICANS (OR 2.4). LDH >2x ULN: median PFS 4.2 months vs 14.8 months for normal LDH. SPD >50 cm²: ORR 58% vs 78% for SPD <50 cm². Metabolic tumor volume (MTV) by PET-CT: MTV >100 mL predicted median PFS 3.8 months vs 16.2 months for MTV <100 mL (HR 3.1). Combined LDH + MTV score identifies ultra-high-risk patients (20% of cohort) with 12-month PFS of 12%. These patients may benefit from bridging therapy or alternative approaches (bispecific antibodies, allogeneic CAR-T).
predictive_value: LDH >2x ULN: PFS 4.2 vs 14.8 months
associated_outcome: CRS severity, response rate, PFS

text_summary: Soluble BCMA (sBCMA) as myeloma response biomarker: BCMA is cleaved from myeloma cell surface by gamma-secretase, producing soluble BCMA detectable in serum. Baseline sBCMA correlates with tumor burden (r=0.72 with M-protein). Post CAR-T: sBCMA nadir at median day 28; sBCMA <2.5 ng/mL at day 28 predicted sustained CR (PPV 0.78). sBCMA rise from nadir preceded clinical relapse by median 2.1 months. Continuous monitoring enables early intervention. Gamma-secretase inhibitor (GSI) pre-treatment: reduces sBCMA 80% and increases surface BCMA 2.5-fold, improving CAR-T recognition. sBCMA also acts as decoy: saturates CAR binding capacity without triggering T-cell activation, contributing to resistance.
predictive_value: sBCMA <2.5: PPV 0.78 for sustained CR
associated_outcome: Response depth, early relapse detection

text_summary: Ferritin trajectory for CRS severity prediction: Serial ferritin measurements starting pre-infusion. Ferritin doubling time <12h in first 48h predicts grade ≥3 CRS with 85% sensitivity and 70% specificity. Absolute ferritin thresholds: >1,500 at 24h (grade ≥2 CRS likely); >5,000 (grade ≥3 CRS likely); >10,000 (HLH/MAS evaluation mandatory). Ferritin >50,000 associated with 40% mortality if not treated aggressively. Recommended monitoring: baseline, then q12h for first 72h, then daily through day 14. Ferritin trajectory (rate of rise) more predictive than single measurement. Combination with CRP (>10 mg/dL) improves prediction: dual elevation at 24h predicts grade ≥3 CRS with 92% sensitivity.
assay_method: Serum ferritin (ng/mL), measured q12h during CRS risk period
clinical_cutoff: Doubling time <12h at 48h; absolute >5000 for grade ≥3
predictive_value: Ferritin doubling <12h: 85% sensitivity for grade ≥3 CRS
associated_outcome: CRS severity prediction, HLH/MAS screening

text_summary: IL-6 kinetics for optimal tocilizumab timing: Serial IL-6 monitoring reveals distinct kinetic patterns. Pattern A (monophasic rise, peak day 3-5): typical grade 1-2 CRS, usually self-resolving. Pattern B (biphasic with second rise): predicts grade ≥3 CRS, intervention at second rise inflection point optimal. Absolute IL-6 >100 pg/mL at 24h: 89% sensitivity for grade ≥3 CRS. Post-tocilizumab: serum IL-6 rises paradoxically (receptor blockade prevents clearance) — this is expected and should NOT trigger additional tocilizumab. Free (non-receptor-bound) IL-6 assay is more informative post-tocilizumab but not widely available. Alternative: sgp130 levels correlate with IL-6 trans-signaling activity. Optimal timing: preemptive tocilizumab at IL-6 >100 pg/mL (24h) reduces grade ≥3 CRS from 18% to 5%.
clinical_cutoff: IL-6 >100 pg/mL at 24h triggers preemptive tocilizumab
predictive_value: Preemptive tocilizumab at IL-6 >100: reduces grade ≥3 CRS 18%→5%
associated_outcome: CRS management timing optimization

text_summary: Angiopoietin-2/Angiopoietin-1 ratio for ICANS prediction: Endothelial activation is central to ICANS pathophysiology (blood-brain barrier disruption). Ang-2/Ang-1 ratio >3.0 at baseline predicts grade ≥3 ICANS with 79% sensitivity and 75% specificity. Ang-2 rises during CRS reflecting endothelial destabilization. Combined with baseline thrombocytopenia (<150K): dual risk factor present → 42% risk of grade ≥3 ICANS vs 5% with neither. Mechanism: Ang-2 displaces Ang-1 from Tie-2 receptor, increasing vascular permeability and enabling cytokine transit across BBB. Prophylactic approach: high Ang-2/Ang-1 ratio patients receive prophylactic dexamethasone (10mg pre-infusion + day 0-2), reducing ICANS grade ≥3 from 15% to 5%.
predictive_value: Ang-2/Ang-1 >3.0: sensitivity 79%, specificity 75%
associated_outcome: ICANS severity prediction

text_summary: CD19 antigen density and relapse prediction: Quantitative CD19 measurement by MESF (molecules of equivalent soluble fluorochrome). Pre-treatment CD19 density on malignant B cells varies 100-fold between patients. CD19 MESF <1,000/cell: high risk for CD19-dim relapse (45% of relapses). CD19 MESF >10,000/cell: antigen loss relapse rare (<5%). Dim CD19 (1,000-5,000 MESF): intermediate risk; correlates with trogocytosis-mediated antigen transfer and reversible CD19 loss. Post-treatment monitoring: CD19+ B-cell recovery with CD19-dim population >20% may indicate emerging resistance clone. Implications for product design: higher-affinity scFv or bispecific (CD19/CD22) for low-density CD19.
clinical_cutoff: MESF >10,000: low relapse risk; <1,000: high CD19-dim relapse risk
predictive_value: MESF <1000: 45% CD19-dim relapse; >10,000: <5% antigen loss
associated_outcome: Antigen loss relapse, product selection

text_summary: Pre-infusion lymphocyte fitness markers at apheresis: T-cell quality at apheresis collection predicts both manufacturing success and clinical outcomes. Key markers: absolute lymphocyte count ≥300/uL (manufacturing failure risk 5x higher if <300); CD3+CD45RA+CCR7+ (naive T-cell) >15% of CD3+ (predicts good expansion); PD-1+TIM-3+ co-expression <20% of CD3+ (low exhaustion); mitochondrial mass (MitoTracker) in upper quartile predicts superior expansion. Prior therapy impact: bendamustine within 6 months reduces naive T-cells 3-fold; anti-CD38 within 3 months depletes T-cells via CDC; checkpoint inhibitor within 3 months paradoxically improves T-cell fitness. Recommendation: optimize apheresis timing — minimum 2-week washout from lymphotoxic agents; apheresis during ALC recovery phase.
predictive_value: ALC <300: 5x manufacturing failure risk
associated_outcome: Manufacturing success, CAR-T expansion, clinical response

text_summary: Lymphodepletion adequacy biomarker (ALC nadir): The depth of lymphodepletion achieved by conditioning chemotherapy (typically fludarabine 30mg/m2 x3 + cyclophosphamide 500mg/m2 x3) directly impacts CAR-T expansion. ALC nadir (typically day -1 or day 0): <100/uL optimal for homeostatic expansion. ALC nadir >500/uL: 3-fold lower Cmax, 50% lower ORR. Mechanism: lymphodepletion creates cytokine sinks (IL-7, IL-15, IL-21 become available), eliminates regulatory T cells, and creates physical space in lymphoid niches. Enhanced lymphodepletion (adding anti-CD52 as in ALLO-501A): further improves CAR-T expansion 3-fold. Monitoring: daily CBC during conditioning to verify ALC trajectory. If ALC >500 on day 0, consider delayed infusion or supplemental cyclophosphamide.
assay_method: Complete blood count, absolute lymphocyte count
predictive_value: ALC >500: 3-fold lower Cmax, 50% lower ORR
associated_outcome: CAR-T expansion, overall response

text_summary: Bone marrow cellularity recovery kinetics: Post CAR-T bone marrow recovery is an important long-term outcome predictor. Assessment at day 30, 60, 90, and 6 months. Cellularity <20% at day 30: 42% risk of prolonged cytopenias (>90 days) vs 8% if cellularity >20%. Megakaryocyte absence at day 30: 65% require prolonged platelet support. Trilineage recovery by day 60: 80% probability of full immune reconstitution by 12 months. Persistent hypocellularity (>90 days): evaluate for secondary MDS/AML (incidence ~2% at 2 years post CAR-T, likely therapy-related rather than CAR-T-related). Iron overload from transfusion dependence: monitor ferritin, consider chelation if >1000 and ongoing transfusion need. Recovery enhanced by: TPO-RA (eltrombopag), G-CSF after day 14, erythropoietin for persistent anemia.
assay_method: Bone marrow biopsy with cellularity assessment
clinical_cutoff: Cellularity >20% at day 30; trilineage recovery by day 60
predictive_value: Cellularity <20% at d30: 42% prolonged cytopenia risk
associated_outcome: Prolonged cytopenias, immune reconstitution
